frameworks.name: valence-arousal model
frameworks.one_line: Two-dimensional affective space with valence and arousal axes.

frameworks.name: double dissociation
frameworks.one_line: OFC subregions track valence while amygdala preferentially tracks arousal.

frameworks.name: bipolar valence continuum
frameworks.one_line: Single bipolar negative-to-positive axis fails to fit neural data.

frameworks.name: hedonic-value (distance-from-neutral) model
frameworks.one_line: Valence as absolute distance from neutral, supported by neural responses.

core_question: Do orbitofrontal cortex and amygdala dissociably encode valence versus arousal, and which model of valence best fits neural activity?
key_assumptions: emotion decomposes into valence and arousal dimensions
affective word stimuli reliably evoke valence and arousal responses
BOLD signal in OFC and amygdala can index affective dimension coding
manipulations: arousal level of affective word stimuli
alternative valence parameterizations (bipolar vs independent vs distance-from-neutral)
key_findings: OFC subregions correlate with valence whereas amygdala preferentially correlates with arousal
neural data support independent positive/negative axes for valence
neural data support absolute distance from neutral as a valence descriptor
neural data fail to support a bipolar valence continuum
interpretations: OFC/amygdala double dissociation for valence and arousal extends from chemosensory to lexical-affective domain
valence is better described by independent axes or hedonic distance than by a bipolar continuum
limitations_noted: title metadata in source mismatches abstract content
